sections.heading: 0. Frame
sections.body: Two features: (1) every workstream gets a unique ID that is its PRIMARY KEY, generated BY THE STORE (not by lume); and (2) a SEED bootstrap workstream that builds repo/project context. Operator decisions baked in: ID (not slug) is the unique key - this aligns the local store with how real PM backings (GitHub Issues, Jira) work, where the backing mints the id; lume must therefore NOT generate ids itself - it asks the store. For the filesystem store the id is a sequential number (seed=0, regular from 1), rendered into the folder name so workstreams sort sequentially on disk. Givens: schema-validated JSON state, dependency-free, deterministic, existing workstreams migrated + renumbered + renamed by creation order. No engine code this iteration.

sections.heading: 1. ID as primary key, generated by the store (the pivot)
sections.body:
Today identity IS the slug everywhere: the TrackingStore is slug-keyed (read/write/has/list + create_workstream(slug)); Repository._load_workstream(slug)/workstream(slug); Workstream.name == slug; FilesystemStore._dir(slug) == root/slug. This was the swappable-backing contract (its decision F3: per-artifact keyed by (slug, artifact)).
Pivot: the STORE owns identity. create becomes 'store mints an id and returns it'; read/write/has are keyed by that id; list returns ids. slug demotes to a human-readable LABEL stored in the workstream record (and used for folder readability), not the key. Rationale (operator): external backings generate their own ids (GH issue number, Jira PROJ-123) - lume cannot pre-assign them, so id generation MUST live behind the store seam. The filesystem store's id scheme is the sequential number; a GH/Jira store's id is whatever the service returns. lume treats the id as an opaque scalar (string) so any backing fits; only FilesystemStore knows its ids are sequential.

sections.heading: 2. Filesystem store id scheme + folder naming
sections.body: FilesystemStore mints ids as a zero-padded sequential number: seed reserved 0, regular = max(existing ids)+1, width min 4 (0000, 0001, ... no hard cap -> thousands). The folder name encodes it: `NNNN-<slug>` (e.g. 0000-seed, 0001-build-lume, 0007-seed-and-numbering) so the filesystem lists workstreams in sequence. The store maps id<->dir (the dir IS the id's home; slug is just in the name for humans). list() reads the numeric prefixes; read/write/has(id) resolve to the `NNNN-*` dir. create_workstream allocates the next number, makes the dir, and returns the id.

sections.heading: 3. Migration of the existing workstreams
sections.body: Six exist, slug-keyed, no stored id and colliding iteration dates. Assign ids by explicit creation order (deterministic, one-time): build-lume=1, lifecycle=2, state-as-data=3, swappable-backing=4, agent-friendly-cli=5, cli-refactoring=6, seed-and-numbering=7 (seed=0 when created). Migration: write the id (+ keep slug as label) into each workstream record, and `git mv` each folder to `NNNN-<slug>` (history-preserving, one-shot), then verify the store resolves every id to its renamed dir. Natural home: `lume migrate` or a dedicated numbering migration.

sections.heading: 4. TrackingStore contract impact (this REVISES swappable-backing)
sections.body: This is a genuine contract change, not just an attribute: the seam moves from slug-keyed to id-keyed with store-minted ids. Concretely the contract becomes ~ create_workstream(slug, title) -> id (store assigns); has(id); read(id, artifact); write(id, artifact, doc); list() -> [id]. FilesystemStore: id = sequential number, dir = NNNN-slug. SQLite store: id = a rows-table id (autoincrement or its own sequence) - it does NOT need folders, so 'sequential on the filesystem' simply doesn't apply; the id still orders listings. In-memory: a counter. A future GitHub/Jira store returns the service id. Repository stops computing numbers (it no longer can) and threads the store-returned id. This supersedes swappable-backing decision F3 (slug-keyed); log that explicitly. NOTE: the swappable-backing contract-matrix tests and Repository will need updating - this is the largest blast radius of the workstream and must stay behaviour-preserving for the user-facing CLI.

sections.heading: 5. -w / resolution + Workstream identity
sections.body: With id as key, Repository.workstream() resolves by id. Operator-facing convenience: -w should accept the id AND the slug-label (slug resolves by scanning for a unique match) so humans aren't forced to type numbers; an ambiguous or missing match is a named error. Workstream identity is the id; slug is a label attribute. status/queue order by id. Open fork: is -w-by-slug in v1 or deferred (id-only first)?

sections.heading: 6. Seed design (id 0)
sections.body: Seed is the special bootstrap workstream; in the filesystem store it is id 0 (the store reserves 0 for it). Portably, seed-ness is also a workstream ATTRIBUTE (e.g. a kind/flag) so a non-sequential backing can mark the seed without literally owning a 0. A `lume seed [--new|--existing]` verb reuses create_workstream + open_iteration(discovery) - no parallel flow - asking the store for the seed id. NEW project -> discovery captures why/scope/constraints/done-when; EXISTING repo -> a map of what the repo contains (languages, layout, entry points, seams). Detection: --new/--existing flag with an emptiness-based default. Seed's output is the context that informs workstream id 1.

sections.heading: 7. Open forks for planning
sections.body:
F1 id type: opaque string in the contract (recommend, for GH/Jira fit); FilesystemStore uses the zero-padded number as its id string.
F2 fs id allocation: scan-for-max vs persisted counter (recommend scan for fs).
F3 width/render: min 4, no cap.
F4 contract reshape: exact new TrackingStore signatures (create->id; id-keyed read/write/has/list) and how Repository threads the id; how much can stay behaviour-preserving.
F5 SQLite/in-memory id schemes under the new contract; update the swappable-backing contract matrix tests.
F6 -w accepts id and/or slug-label; v1 or defer slug resolution.
F7 seed surface: `lume seed` verb + a seed attribute/kind for portability; reserved fs id 0.
F8 new-vs-existing detection + exact seed discovery content per mode.
F9 folder rename migration: git mv safety, one-shot, post-rename verification; home in `lume migrate` vs dedicated.
F10 phasing: (a) id-as-key contract change + fs numbering + folder rename + migration, then (b) seed - recommend separate slices, (a) first.

sections.heading: 8. Scope held / deferred
sections.body: No engine code this iteration; artifact is discovery.json only. This pivot (id primary key, store-minted) is larger than the original numbering framing because it revises the TrackingStore contract from swappable-backing - planning must size that carefully and keep the CLI behaviour-preserving. Deferred to planning: the exact contract signatures, id type, -w resolution, seed surface, detection, and phasing. Seed (id 0) presupposes the id/contract work, so sequence it after.
